title: West Midlands (county)

infobox.value: [28 MPs](/wiki/List_of_parliamentary_constituencies_in_the_West_Midlands_(county) "List of parliamentary constituencies in the West Midlands (county)")
[42nd of 48](/wiki/List_of_ceremonial_counties_of_England "List of ceremonial counties of England")

1. [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton") 2. [Dudley](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Dudley "Metropolitan Borough of Dudley") 3. [Walsall](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Walsall "Metropolitan Borough of Walsall") 4. [Sandwell](/wiki/Sandwell "Sandwell") 5. [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") 6. [Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull") 7. [Coventry](/wiki/City_of_Coventry "City of Coventry")

is a [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county") and [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the larger [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") region of England. It is bordered by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") to the north and west and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") to the south. To the east it projects into [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), which surrounds the projection on all sides except the west. The largest settlement is the city of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham").

**West Midlands** is a [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county") and [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England") in the larger [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") region of England. It is bordered by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") to the north and west and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") to the south. To the east it projects into [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire"), which surrounds the projection on all sides except the west. The largest settlement is the city of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham").

The county has an area of 902 km2 (348 sq mi) and is almost entirely urban, with an estimated population of 3,036,605 in 2024; this made it the 2nd [most populous county](/wiki/List_of_ceremonial_counties_of_England "List of ceremonial counties of England") in England after [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London"). Birmingham is in the centre of the county, with [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull") and the city of [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") to the east and [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich") and the city of [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton") to the west. [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield") lies to the north. Most of the settlements in the centre and west of the county belong to the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation"), which is separated from Coventry by [a rural area](/wiki/Meriden_Gap "Meriden Gap") around the town of [Balsall Common](/wiki/Balsall_Common "Balsall Common"). For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") purposes the West Midlands comprises seven [metropolitan boroughs](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough"): Birmingham, Coventry, [Dudley](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Dudley "Metropolitan Borough of Dudley"), [Sandwell](/wiki/Sandwell "Sandwell"), [Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull"), [Walsall](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Walsall "Metropolitan Borough of Walsall"), and Wolverhampton. Their councils collaborate through the [West Midlands Combined Authority](/wiki/West_Midlands_Combined_Authority "West Midlands Combined Authority"). The county was historically part of [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire_(Detached) "Shropshire (Detached)"), Staffordshire, Warwickshire, and Worcestershire.

The west of the county encloses the valley of the [River Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands") and its tributaries; the highest point of the surrounding area is [Turners Hill](/wiki/Turners_Hill,_West_Midlands "Turners Hill, West Midlands"), at 271 metres (889 ft). West Midlands contains the [Sutton Park](/wiki/Sutton_Park,_West_Midlands "Sutton Park, West Midlands") [Site of Special Scientific Interest](/wiki/List_of_Sites_of_Special_Scientific_Interest_in_the_West_Midlands "List of Sites of Special Scientific Interest in the West Midlands"), which has an area of 970 hectares (2,400 acres) and is one of the largest urban parks in Europe. The area between Solihull and Coventry is part of the [Forest of Arden](/wiki/Forest_of_Arden "Forest of Arden"), and the rivers [Sowe](/wiki/River_Sowe "River Sowe") and [Sherbourne](/wiki/River_Sherbourne "River Sherbourne") flow through Coventry.

## Status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Status")]

The metropolitan county exists in law, as a geographical frame of reference,[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) and as a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England"). As such it has a [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_the_West_Midlands "Lord Lieutenant of the West Midlands")[[7]](#cite_note-7) and a [High Sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff "High Sheriff").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Between 1974 and 1986, the [West Midlands County Council](/wiki/West_Midlands_County_Council "West Midlands County Council") was the administrative body covering the county; this was abolished on 31 March 1986, and the constituent metropolitan boroughs effectively became [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"). A new administrative body for the county (and some of the district surrounding it as Non-Constituent members), the [West Midlands Combined Authority](/wiki/West_Midlands_Combined_Authority "West Midlands Combined Authority"), was created in June 2016. Since May 2017, the authority has been headed by a directly elected [Mayor of the West Midlands](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_West_Midlands "Mayor of the West Midlands"), a position currently held by [Richard Parker](/wiki/Richard_Parker_(mayor) "Richard Parker (mayor)") of the [Labour and Co-operative Parties](/wiki/Labour_and_Co-operative_Party "Labour and Co-operative Party"). Other county-wide bodies include the [West Midlands Police](/wiki/West_Midlands_Police "West Midlands Police"), the [West Midlands Fire Service](/wiki/West_Midlands_Fire_Service "West Midlands Fire Service") and [Transport for West Midlands](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands").

The county is sometimes described as the "West Midlands [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area")" or the "[West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation")" or "Greater Birmingham", although these have different, less clearly defined, boundaries. The main [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") or urban area does not include Coventry, for example. The name "West Midlands" is also used for the much larger [West Midlands region](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)"), which sometimes causes confusion. Geographically the county is on the eastern side of the region, the western side comprising [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") and [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire") and the southern side comprising [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") and [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the West Midlands](/wiki/History_of_the_West_Midlands "History of the West Midlands")

See also: [History of Birmingham](/wiki/History_of_Birmingham "History of Birmingham") and [History of Coventry](/wiki/History_of_Coventry "History of Coventry")

Although the modern county has only existed since 1974, the settlements of the West Midlands have long been important centres of commerce and industry as well as developing a good local infrastructure. Coventry was one of England's most important cities during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), with its prosperity built upon wool and cloth manufacture. Birmingham and Wolverhampton have a tradition of industry dating back to the 16th century, when small metal-working industries developed. Birmingham was known for its manufacture of [small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms"), whereas Wolverhampton became a centre of lock manufacture and brass working. The coal and iron ore deposits of the [Black Country](/wiki/Black_Country "Black Country") area provided a ready source of raw materials. The area grew rapidly during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), and by the 20th century had grown into one large conurbation. Coventry was slower to develop, but by the early 20th century it had become an important centre of bicycle and car manufacture.

1966 saw a substantial reform in the local government of the area as the patchwork of [county boroughs](/wiki/County_Borough "County Borough") with [municipal boroughs](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough") and [urban district councils](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") in between was replaced by a core of county boroughs covering a contiguous area, roughly as follows:

* [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), which remained substantially unaltered
* [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley"), which absorbed all [Brierley Hill](/wiki/Brierley_Hill "Brierley Hill"), most of [Coseley](/wiki/Coseley "Coseley") and [Sedgley](/wiki/Sedgley "Sedgley"), and part of [Amblecote](/wiki/Amblecote "Amblecote"), [Tipton](/wiki/Tipton "Tipton") and [Rowley Regis](/wiki/Rowley_Regis "Rowley Regis")
* [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull"), which remained substantially unaltered
* [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"), which absorbed all [Darlaston](/wiki/Darlaston "Darlaston"), most of [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall"), and parts of [Wednesbury](/wiki/Wednesbury "Wednesbury"), [Coseley](/wiki/Coseley "Coseley"), [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") and [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston")
* [Warley](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Warley "County Borough of Warley"), which was created by amalgamating most of [Smethwick](/wiki/Smethwick "Smethwick"), [Oldbury](/wiki/Oldbury,_West_Midlands "Oldbury, West Midlands") and [Rowley Regis](/wiki/Rowley_Regis "Rowley Regis"), and parts of Dudley, Tipton, West Bromwich and [Halesowen](/wiki/Halesowen "Halesowen")
* [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich"), which absorbed most of Wednesbury and Tipton, and parts of [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston"), Oldbury, [Smethwick](/wiki/Smethwick "Smethwick") and Walsall
* [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton"), which absorbed most of [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston"), [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") and [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall "Tettenhall"), and parts of Sedgley, Coseley and Willenhall

Near the area, three other towns remained separate ([Halesowen](/wiki/Halesowen "Halesowen"), [Stourbridge](/wiki/Stourbridge "Stourbridge") and [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield")), while [Aldridge](/wiki/Aldridge "Aldridge") and [Brownhills](/wiki/Brownhills "Brownhills") joined to form a single unit, called [Aldridge-Brownhills](/wiki/Aldridge-Brownhills_Urban_District "Aldridge-Brownhills Urban District"). In the same year, a single [West Midlands Constabulary](/wiki/West_Midlands_Constabulary "West Midlands Constabulary") was formed for the Black Country county boroughs, whilst Birmingham retained its [Birmingham City Police](/wiki/Birmingham_City_Police "Birmingham City Police") and Solihull continued being policed by the [Warwickshire Constabulary](/wiki/Warwickshire_Constabulary "Warwickshire Constabulary"). The [West Midlands Passenger Transport Authority](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands") was established in 1968.

### County creation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: County creation")]

In 1974, the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972") came into effect, creating the metropolitan county of West Midlands. This area was based on the seven county boroughs and the other non-county boroughs and urban districts around the fringe of the conurbation.

The new area consisted of seven new metropolitan boroughs, with [Aldridge-Brownhills](/wiki/Aldridge-Brownhills_Urban_District "Aldridge-Brownhills Urban District") added to [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"); Halesowen and Stourbridge to Dudley and Sutton Coldfield to Birmingham. A new borough of [Sandwell](/wiki/Sandwell "Sandwell") was formed by the merger of West Bromwich and Warley. The actual designation of Warley itself was abolished and the three towns of Smethwick, Oldbury and Rowley Regis reinstated as component parts of Sandwell, although these areas formed the Warley postal district. Solihull took in much of the suburban fringe to the east of Birmingham, including the former villages of [Chelmsley Wood](/wiki/Chelmsley_Wood "Chelmsley Wood") and [Castle Bromwich](/wiki/Castle_Bromwich "Castle Bromwich"), also [Birmingham Airport](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport"), and the area of countryside between Solihull and Coventry, whilst Coventry itself received only small changes and Wolverhampton was unaltered. This led to (apart from in the east, with Coventry and the Meriden Gap) quite a tightly defined metropolitan border, excluding such places as [Burntwood](/wiki/Burntwood "Burntwood"), [Bromsgrove](/wiki/Bromsgrove "Bromsgrove"), [Cannock](/wiki/Cannock "Cannock"), [Kidderminster](/wiki/Kidderminster "Kidderminster"), [Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield "Lichfield") and [Wombourne](/wiki/Wombourne "Wombourne") which had been considered for inclusion in the West Midlands metropolitan area by the [Redcliffe-Maud Report](/wiki/Redcliffe-Maud_Report "Redcliffe-Maud Report").

The 1974 reform created the [West Midlands County Council](/wiki/West_Midlands_County_Council "West Midlands County Council") that covered the entire area and dealt with strategic issues. A new [West Midlands Police](/wiki/West_Midlands_Police "West Midlands Police") service was formed covering the entire area, with the West Midlands Constabulary and Birmingham City Police abolished, and also taking over responsibility from the county forces.

West Midlands was also established as a new [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_county "Ceremonial county"), with the offices of [Lord Lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_the_West_Midlands "Lord Lieutenant of the West Midlands") and [High Sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_the_West_Midlands "High Sheriff of the West Midlands") created. Its constituent components had previously been, for ceremonial purposes, under the equivalent offices of [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") ([Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") [CB](/wiki/County_borough "County borough"), [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") CB, [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull") CB, [Sutton Coldfield](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield "Sutton Coldfield") [MB](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough") and [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden_Rural_District "Meriden Rural District") [RD](/wiki/Rural_district "Rural district")), [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") ([Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton") CB, [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall") CB, [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich") CB, [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley") CB and Aldridge-Brownhills [UD](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)")) and [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") ([Warley](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Warley "County Borough of Warley") CB, [Stourbridge](/wiki/Stourbridge "Stourbridge") MB and [Halesowen](/wiki/Halesowen "Halesowen") MB).

### West Midlands County Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: West Midlands County Council")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Arms_of_West_Midlands_Metropolitan_County_Council.svg/250px-Arms_of_West_Midlands_Metropolitan_County_Council.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arms_of_West_Midlands_Metropolitan_County_Council.svg)

The arms of the [West Midlands County Council](/wiki/West_Midlands_County_Council "West Midlands County Council"), depicted here, became redundant with the abolition of the council in 1986 (though similar arms are used by the [West Midlands Fire Service](/wiki/West_Midlands_Fire_Service "West Midlands Fire Service")).

Between 1974 and 1986, the county had a two-tier system of local government, and the seven districts shared power with the [West Midlands County Council](/wiki/West_Midlands_County_Council "West Midlands County Council"). However, the [Local Government Act 1985](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1985 "Local Government Act 1985") abolished the metropolitan county councils, and the West Midlands County Council ceased to exist in 1986. Most of its functions were devolved to the West Midland boroughs, which effectively became [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"), with responsibility for most local authority functions.

Following the abolition of the county council, some county-wide bodies continued to exist, which were administered by various joint-boards of the seven districts, among these were the [West Midlands Police](/wiki/West_Midlands_Police "West Midlands Police"), the [West Midlands Fire Service](/wiki/West_Midlands_Fire_Service "West Midlands Fire Service") and the [West Midlands Passenger Transport Executive](/wiki/West_Midlands_Passenger_Transport_Executive "West Midlands Passenger Transport Executive").

### Boundary changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Boundary changes")]

In 1994, the western/southern shores of [Chasewater](/wiki/Chasewater "Chasewater"), plus the adjacent Jeffreys Swag, were transferred from the [Metropolitan Borough of Walsall](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Walsall "Metropolitan Borough of Walsall") to the [District of Lichfield](/wiki/Lichfield_(district) "Lichfield (district)"), Staffordshire.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Further boundary changes came into effect in 1995, when part of the [Hereford and Worcester](/wiki/Hereford_and_Worcester "Hereford and Worcester") parish of [Frankley](/wiki/Frankley "Frankley") (including the south-west part of [Bartley Reservoir](/wiki/Bartley_Reservoir "Bartley Reservoir")) was transferred to Birmingham and became part of the county.

### West Midlands Combined Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: West Midlands Combined Authority")]

On 17 June 2016, a new administrative body, the [West Midlands Combined Authority](/wiki/West_Midlands_Combined_Authority "West Midlands Combined Authority") was created for the county, under the [Local Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009](/wiki/Local_Democracy,_Economic_Development_and_Construction_Act_2009 "Local Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009"), which created several other [combined authorities](/wiki/Combined_authority "Combined authority") in England. The new body has powers over transport, economic development, skills and planning. A new directly elected position of [Mayor of the West Midlands](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_West_Midlands "Mayor of the West Midlands") was created in 2017 to chair the new body.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The first [Mayoral election](/wiki/2017_West_Midlands_mayoral_election "2017 West Midlands mayoral election") was held in May 2017, and the position was won by [Andy Street](/wiki/Andy_Street "Andy Street") of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)").

Population density map

The West Midlands is a landlocked county that borders the counties of [Warwickshire](/wiki/Warwickshire "Warwickshire") to the east, [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire") to the south, and [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") to the north and west.

The West Midlands County is one of the most heavily urbanised counties in the UK. [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton "Wolverhampton"), the [Black Country](/wiki/Black_Country "Black Country") and [Solihull](/wiki/Solihull "Solihull") together form the [third most populous conurbation in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom") with a combined population of around 2.44 million.[[12]](#cite_note-BUA2011-12) However, the West Midlands is not entirely urban; [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") is separated from the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation") by a stretch of [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt_(UK) "Green belt (UK)") land approximately 13 miles (21 km) across, known as the "[Meriden Gap](/wiki/Meriden_Gap "Meriden Gap")", which retains a strongly rural character. A smaller piece of green belt between Birmingham, Walsall and West Bromwich includes [Barr Beacon](/wiki/Barr_Beacon "Barr Beacon") and the [Sandwell Valley](/wiki/Sandwell_Valley "Sandwell Valley").

The highest point in the West Midlands is [Turners Hill](/wiki/Turners_Hill,_West_Midlands "Turners Hill, West Midlands"), with an elevation of 271 m (889 ft).[[13]](#cite_note-13) The hill is a [Site of Special Scientific Interest](/wiki/Site_of_Special_Scientific_Interest "Site of Special Scientific Interest"). [Barr Beacon](/wiki/Barr_Beacon "Barr Beacon") is another hill in the West Midlands, located on the border of Birmingham and Walsall, with a height of 227 metres (745 ft).

There are 23 [Sites of Special Scientific Interest](/wiki/List_of_Sites_of_Special_Scientific_Interest_in_the_West_Midlands "List of Sites of Special Scientific Interest in the West Midlands") in the county.[[14]](#cite_note-14) One of these SSSIs is [Sutton Park](/wiki/Sutton_Park,_West_Midlands "Sutton Park, West Midlands") in Sutton Coldfield, which has an area of 970 hectares (2,400 acres).[[15]](#cite_note-15) As a result, it is one of the largest urban parks in Europe, and the largest outside of a capital city in Europe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The park also has [national nature reserve](/wiki/National_nature_reserve_(United_Kingdom) "National nature reserve (United Kingdom)") status.

There are numerous rivers that pass through the county, including the [River Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands"). The [river basin](/wiki/River_basin "River basin") is the most urbanised basin in the United Kingdom, with approximately 42% of the basin being urbanised.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The River Tame is fed by the [River Rea](/wiki/River_Rea "River Rea"), [River Anker](/wiki/River_Anker "River Anker"), and the [River Blythe](/wiki/River_Blythe "River Blythe"), which in turn is fed by the [River Cole](/wiki/River_Cole,_West_Midlands "River Cole, West Midlands"). The [River Sowe](/wiki/River_Sowe "River Sowe") and [River Sherbourne](/wiki/River_Sherbourne "River Sherbourne") both flow through Coventry. The [River Stour](/wiki/River_Stour,_Worcestershire "River Stour, Worcestershire") flows through the west of the West Midlands county.

Like other [metropolitan counties](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county"), the West Midlands is divided into [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_England "Districts of England") called [metropolitan boroughs](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough"). There are seven boroughs in the West Midlands, six of which are named after the largest settlement in their administrative area. The West Midlands is unusual amongst the metropolitan counties in that three of its boroughs have [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"); Coventry is a city by [ancient prescriptive usage](/wiki/Time_immemorial "Time immemorial"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) Birmingham was granted city status in 1889,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and Wolverhampton in 2000 as a "Millennium City".[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The [National Exhibition Centre](/wiki/National_Exhibition_Centre "National Exhibition Centre") (NEC) is just north. [ZF Lemförder UK](/wiki/ZF_Friedrichshafen "ZF Friedrichshafen")'s site at Bickenhill makes axle modules for Land Rover. [Newey and Eyre](/wiki/Newey_and_Eyre "Newey and Eyre"), Britain's largest electrical wholesaler, is at Sheldon. Neopost UK is off the A452. [Goodrich UK](/wiki/Goodrich_Corporation "Goodrich Corporation") is in [Shirley](/wiki/Shirley,_West_Midlands "Shirley, West Midlands"). [TRW Conekt](/wiki/TRW_Automotive "TRW Automotive") have a main automotive engineering research centre at The Green Business Park in Shirley Heath. The Mormons (Latter Day Saints) have their European HQ in Solihull.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Former_Staffordshire_Building_Society_Offices.jpg/250px-Former_Staffordshire_Building_Society_Offices.jpg)](/wiki/File:Former_Staffordshire_Building_Society_Offices.jpg)

Carillion head office in Wolverhampton

The UK's [VAT Registration Service](/w/index.php?title=VAT_Registration_Service&action=edit&redlink=1 "VAT Registration Service (page does not exist)"), for [Value Added Tax](/wiki/Value_Added_Tax_(United_Kingdom) "Value Added Tax (United Kingdom)") is at HMRC in Wolverhampton. [Flint Ink](/wiki/Flint_Group "Flint Group") UK in the east of the town centre, was the largest ink supplier in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), before being bought in 1998.

[Turner Powertrain Systems](/w/index.php?title=Turner_Powertrain_Systems&action=edit&redlink=1 "Turner Powertrain Systems (page does not exist)") is the world market leader for transmissions for [backhoe loaders](/wiki/Backhoe_loader "Backhoe loader"), mini [excavators](/wiki/Excavator "Excavator") [telescopic handlers](/wiki/Telescopic_handler "Telescopic handler"), and site dumpers is further south, near [Dunstall Hill](/wiki/Dunstall_Hill "Dunstall Hill"). Tata Steel (former site of British Steel Seamless Tubes until 1995) have their [Wednesfield Steelpark](/w/index.php?title=Wednesfield_Steelpark&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wednesfield Steelpark (page does not exist)") (built in 1999) on the Walsall boundary.

[Essar Steel](/wiki/Essar_Group "Essar Group") UK in west Dudley, is the largest independent steel toll processor in the UK.

[Hadley Group](/wiki/Hadley_Group "Hadley Group") near the Soho Foundry is the largest [cold roll forming](/wiki/Rolling_(metalworking) "Rolling (metalworking)") manufacturer in the UK. Caparo Precision Tubes in Oldbury, is the UK's largest producer of [electric resistance welded](/wiki/Electric_resistance_welding "Electric resistance welding") (ERW) steel tubes, and [Wellman Group](/wiki/Wellman_Group "Wellman Group") make boilers to the west. [Metsec](/wiki/Metsec "Metsec"), east of Oldbury, is one of the UK's largest [cold roll-forming](/wiki/Rolling_(metalworking) "Rolling (metalworking)") companies. The AA have a main office in [Rounds Green](/w/index.php?title=Rounds_Green&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rounds Green (page does not exist)"), west of Oldbury. [2 Sisters Food Group](/wiki/2_Sisters_Food_Group "2 Sisters Food Group"), Britain's largest processor of chicken, is based in the West Midlands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The national headquarters of [One Stop](/wiki/One_Stop "One Stop") is in [Brownhills](/wiki/Brownhills "Brownhills"), at [Clayhanger](/wiki/Clayhanger,_West_Midlands "Clayhanger, West Midlands"). [Poundland](/wiki/Poundland "Poundland") is in [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall"). [Wedge Group](/wiki/Wedge_Group "Wedge Group"), based in Willenhall, is the largest [hot dip galvanising](/wiki/Hot-dip_galvanizing "Hot-dip galvanizing") company in the UK. [Assa Abloy](/wiki/Assa_Abloy "Assa Abloy") UK (and [Yale](/wiki/Yale_(company) "Yale (company)") UK, former Yale & Towne), is also in Willenhall, as the town is known for manufacture of locks. [A.F. Blakemore](/wiki/A.F._Blakemore "A.F. Blakemore"), supplies most of the [SPAR](/wiki/Spar_(retailer) "Spar (retailer)") shops in the UK. ## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Education")]

The West Midlands contains ten universities, seven of which are in Birmingham:

* [Aston University](/wiki/Aston_University "Aston University")
* [University of Birmingham](/wiki/University_of_Birmingham "University of Birmingham")
* [Birmingham City University](/wiki/Birmingham_City_University "Birmingham City University")
* [University College Birmingham](/wiki/University_College_Birmingham "University College Birmingham")
* [BPP University](/wiki/BPP_University "BPP University")
* [University of Law](/wiki/University_of_Law "University of Law")
* [Birmingham Newman University](/wiki/Birmingham_Newman_University "Birmingham Newman University")

Both [Coventry University](/wiki/Coventry_University "Coventry University") and the [University of Warwick](/wiki/University_of_Warwick "University of Warwick") are in Coventry whilst [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton") is located in Wolverhampton with campuses in [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford") and [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall").

Each of the local authorities has at least one further education college for students aged over 16, and since September 1992 all of the local authorities have operated traditional 5–7 infant, 7–11 junior, and 11-16/18 secondary schools for students in compulsory education. This followed the demise of 5–8 first, 8–12 middle and 12-16/18 secondary schools in the [Sutton Coldfield area](/w/index.php?title=Sutton_Coldfield_area&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sutton Coldfield area (page does not exist)").[[51]](#cite_note-Sutton_Coldfield-53)

For 18 years before September 1990, Dudley had operated 5–8 first, 8–12 middle, and 12-16/18 secondary schools before then, while Halesowen (September 1972 until July 1982) and Aldridge-Brownhills (September 1972 until July 1986) had both operated 5–9 first, 9–13 middle and 13-16/18 secondary schools.

Many local authorities still have sixth form facilities in secondary schools, though sixth form facilities had been axed by most secondary schools in Dudley since the early 1990s (and in Halesowen in 1982) as the local authorities changed direction towards further education colleges.

All secondary state education in Dudley and Sandwell is mixed comprehensive, although there are a small number of single sex and grammar schools existing in parts of Birmingham, Solihull, Wolverhampton and Walsall.

In August 2009, Matthew Boulton College and Sutton Coldfield College merged to become [Birmingham Metropolitan College](/wiki/Birmingham_Metropolitan_College "Birmingham Metropolitan College"), one of the largest further and higher education institutions in the country. Plans are afoot for the construction of a new campus in the [Perry Barr](/wiki/Perry_Barr "Perry Barr") area of Birmingham.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Television")]

The area is served by [BBC West Midlands](/wiki/BBC_West_Midlands "BBC West Midlands") and [ITV Central](/wiki/ITV_Central "ITV Central") which both broadcast from Birmingham, the local based-television station [TalkBirmingham](/wiki/TalkBirmingham "TalkBirmingham") also covers the area. Television signals are received from the [Sutton Coldfield transmitting station](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield_transmitting_station "Sutton Coldfield transmitting station").[[65]](#cite_note-67)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Radio")]

In terms of [BBC Local Radio](/wiki/BBC_Local_Radio "BBC Local Radio"), the area is served by [BBC Radio WM](/wiki/BBC_Radio_WM "BBC Radio WM") and [BBC CWR](/wiki/BBC_CWR "BBC CWR") covering Coventry.

Commercial radio stations include [Hits Radio Birmingham](/wiki/Hits_Radio_Birmingham "Hits Radio Birmingham"), [Capital Midlands](/wiki/Capital_Midlands "Capital Midlands"), [Capital Mid-Counties](/wiki/Capital_Mid-Counties "Capital Mid-Counties"), [Heart West Midlands](/wiki/Heart_West_Midlands "Heart West Midlands"), [Hits Radio Coventry & Warwickshire](/wiki/Hits_Radio_Coventry_%26_Warwickshire "Hits Radio Coventry & Warwickshire"), [Smooth West Midlands](/wiki/Smooth_West_Midlands "Smooth West Midlands"), [Greatest Hits Radio Birmingham & The West Midlands](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_Birmingham_%26_The_West_Midlands "Greatest Hits Radio Birmingham & The West Midlands"), and [Greatest Hits Radio Black Country & Shropshire](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_Black_Country_%26_Shropshire "Greatest Hits Radio Black Country & Shropshire").

[Community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") stations include:

* The Bridge 102.5 (Stourbridge)
* [Big City Radio](/wiki/Big_City_Radio "Big City Radio") (Birmingham)
* Radio Plus and Hillz FM in (Coventry)
* [WCR FM](/wiki/WCR_FM "WCR FM") and Gorgeous Radio (Wolverhampton)
* [Black Country Radio](/wiki/Black_Country_Radio "Black Country Radio") (Black Country)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Midlands_(county)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sport")]

The West Midlands is home to numerous sports teams. In [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), the West Midlands is home to various clubs including [Wasps RFC](/wiki/Wasps_RFC "Wasps RFC"), Birmingham Barbarians, [Sutton Coldfield RFC](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield_RFC "Sutton Coldfield RFC"), [Moseley Rugby Football Club](/wiki/Moseley_Rugby_Football_Club "Moseley Rugby Football Club"), [Birmingham & Solihull RFC](/wiki/Birmingham_%26_Solihull_RFC "Birmingham & Solihull RFC"), and [Coventry RFC](/wiki/Coventry_RFC "Coventry RFC").

In [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), the [Midlands Hurricanes](/wiki/Midlands_Hurricanes "Midlands Hurricanes") are the only team from the county playing in the professional ranks, currently in the third tier [League 1](/wiki/RFL_League_1 "RFL League 1").

In [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), there are six [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") teams in the county of which two, [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), and [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C."), play in the Premier League. The following clubs are often referred to as the West Midlands "Big Six":

| Club | League | City/town | Stadium | Capacity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") | Premier League | Birmingham | [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park") | 42,788 |

| [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") | Championship | West Bromwich | [The Hawthorns](/wiki/The_Hawthorns "The Hawthorns") | 26,500 |
| [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") | Championship | Coventry | [Coventry Building Society Arena](/wiki/Coventry_Building_Society_Arena "Coventry Building Society Arena") | 32,609 |
| [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") | Championship | Birmingham | [St Andrew's](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_(stadium) "St Andrew's (stadium)") | 30,079 |

The West Midlands is also home to [Warwickshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Warwickshire_County_Cricket_Club "Warwickshire County Cricket Club"), who are based at [Edgbaston Cricket Ground](/wiki/Edgbaston_Cricket_Ground "Edgbaston Cricket Ground"), which also hosts [Test matches](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") and [One Day Internationals](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International"). The [Birmingham Panthers](/wiki/Birmingham_Panthers "Birmingham Panthers") basketball team replaced the [Birmingham Bullets](/wiki/Birmingham_Bullets "Birmingham Bullets") and are currently based at a facility provided by the [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton") in Walsall.

The West Midlands has its own [Quidditch](/wiki/Quidditch "Quidditch") team, West Midlands Revolution (after its part in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution")), which won the Quidditch Premier League in 2017.[[66]](#cite_note-68)
